coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for multicolor cell imaging and ultra-sensitive detection of multiple ions in living cells: One Stone for multiple Birds
coredata.dc:creator: Durrani, Samran
Zhang, Jie
Pang, Ai-Ping
Gao, Yichen
Wang, Tian-Yu
Wang, Haiyan
Wu, Fu-Gen
Lin, Fengming
coredata.dc:description: Given the significant impact of ions on environment pollution and human health, it is urgently needed to establish effective and convenient ion detection approaches, particularly in living cells. In this paper, we constructed multicolor N-doped-carbon dots (mPD-CDs) by facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization of m-phenylenediamine (mPD). mPD-CDs were successfully deployed for multicolor cellular imaging for animal cells, fungi, and bacteria in a wash-free way with high photostability and satisfactory biocompability. Moreover, mPD-CDs can be used as a fluorescent sensing probe for ultrasensitive detection of both iodide ion (I−) and typical heavy metals such as cadmium (Cd2+), copper (Cu2+), mercury (Hg2+), gadolinium (Gd3+), ferrous ion (Fe2+), Zinc (Zn2+), and ferric ion (Fe3+). This is the first report using CDs as optical sensing probe for the detection of Gd3+, and for detection of Fe3+ with fluorescence “turn on”. More significantly, with these versatile and fascinating properties, we applied mPD-CDs for intracellular ion detection in living cells like Hep G2 and S. cerevisiae, and zebra fish. Altogether, mPD-CDs displayed great potential for multicolor cell imaging and the multiple ion detection in vitro and in vivo, presenting a promising strategy for in-situ ultrasensitive sensing of multiple metal ions in the environment and the biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Intracellular ion detection
Pollutant
Biosensor
Multicolor bioimaging
Bioprobe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of varied ions on the fluorescence of mPD-CDs
The ion detection in animal cells and microorganisms using mPD-CDs
mPD-CDs for multicolor imaging of animal, fungal and bacterial cells
mPD-CDs as the fluorescence probe for the detection of multiple ions
Application of mPD-CDs for intracellular ion detection
mPD-CDs for sensing multiple ions in zebra fish

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32170072 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities , and Research Innovation Program of International Graduate Students of Southeast University ( SEU ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Profiling of mPD-CDs. (A) TEM imaging of mPD-CDs and (B) their corresponding size distribution. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of mPD-CDs in water. (D) Zeta potential and (E) fluorescence spectra of mPD-CDs in water. (F) FTIR spectrum, and (G) full XPS spectrum of dried mPD-CDs. The high-resolution peaks in the XPS spectrum including (H) C1s, (I) N1s, and (J) O1s were presented.

Confocal images of (A) animal cells (RAW 264.7, Hep G2 and 4T1), (B) fungal cells (C. albicans and S. cerevisiae), and (C) bacterial cells (E. coli and S. aureus) with the treatment of mPD-CDs. The emission wavelengths for blue channel, green channel, and red channel were 410–480 nm, 500–580 nm, and 581–700 nm when excited at 405, 488, and 552 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The effect of (A) temperature and (B) ionic strength on the PL intensity of mPD-CDs. The biocompatibility of mPD-CDs was evaluated by measuring the viabilities of (C) animal cells (4T1 and L929) and (D) fungi (S. cerevisiae and C. albicans) cells treated with mPD-CDs of different concentrations.
The fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs in the presence of varied ions.

(A–D and I-L) Fluorescence emission spectra of mPD-CDs suspensions in the presence of various ions at different concentrations, including Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Cd2+, and Zn2+, respectively. The excitation wavelength was 280 nm. (E-H and M-O) The corresponding linear relationship between the relative fluorescence change (F–F0)/F0 and the ion concentration was presented for each ion.

Imaging and sensing of intracellular ions based on mPD-CDs. (A) CLSM images of Hep G2 and S. cerevisiae treated with different ions. (B) The corresponding cellular fluorescence intensity of all these samples were measured by flow cytometry analysis. (C) The corresponding statistics of the flow cytometric results in (B). The emission wavelength was 410–480 nm excited at 405 nm.

Imaging and sensing of multiple ions based on mPD-CDs in zebra fish. CLSM images of zebra fish treated with different metal ions individually at 200 μM for 2 h and then incubated with 100 μg/mL mPD-CDs for 1.5 h.

Schematic illustration of mPD-CDs synthesis, applications for multiple ions detection, intracellular multiple ions detection using live animal cells (Hep G2) and fungal cells (S. cerevisiae), and multicolor universal cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for multicolor cell imaging and ultra-sensitive detection of multiple ions in living cells: One Stone for multiple Birds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210096, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the significant impact of ions on environment pollution and human health, it is urgently needed to establish effective and convenient ion detection approaches, particularly in living cells. In this paper, we constructed multicolor N-doped-carbon dots (mPD-CDs) by facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization of m-phenylenediamine (mPD). mPD-CDs were successfully deployed for multicolor cellular imaging for animal cells, fungi, and bacteria in a wash-free way with high photostability and satisfactory biocompability. Moreover, mPD-CDs can be used as a fluorescent sensing probe for ultrasensitive detection of both iodide ion (I−) and typical heavy metals such as cadmium (Cd2+), copper (Cu2+), mercury (Hg2+), gadolinium (Gd3+), ferrous ion (Fe2+), Zinc (Zn2+), and ferric ion (Fe3+). This is the first report using CDs as optical sensing probe for the detection of Gd3+, and for detection of Fe3+ with fluorescence “turn on”. More significantly, with these versatile and fascinating properties, we applied mPD-CDs for intracellular ion detection in living cells like Hep G2 and S. cerevisiae, and zebra fish. Altogether, mPD-CDs displayed great potential for multicolor cell imaging and the multiple ion detection in vitro and in vivo, presenting a promising strategy for in-situ ultrasensitive sensing of multiple metal ions in the environment and the biological systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Some Ions are essential for life, playing crucial roles in fundamental processes such as catalysis, osmotic regulation, signalling, and biomineralization (Carter et al., 2014), while some ions are toxic, posing severe threats to human health. Specifically, iron (Fe3+), zinc (Zn2+), copper (Cu2+), and iodide (I−) are ubiquitous and essential elements in all forms of life for tremendous physiological and pathological functions (Carter et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2021), of which the aberrant levels often result in different diseases like anaemia, Wilson's/Alzheimer's/Parkinson's disease, hepatitis, thyroid disorders, neurological damage, or even cancer (Araya et al., 2007; Zeng et al., 2021; Tabaraki and Sadeghinejad, 2018). Gadolinium (Gd3+), a lanthanide element, has been widely used in radio sensitization enhancement and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents (Du et al., 2017). However, Gd3+ might inhibit calcium channels, inducing nephrogenic system fibrosis in patients with renal dysfunction (Ji et al., 2021). Cadmium (Cd2+) and Mercury (Hg2+) are highly toxic and widely distributed in the environment, which can bioaccumulate through the food chain. Even at very low concentrations, Cd2+ may cause varied diseases like cancers, lung damage, renal dysfunction, reproductive defects, and bone fractures (Chen et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2018; Altaf et al., 2022), while Hg2+ may deteriorate immune system and many organs of human body (Ren et al., 2017). Therefore, the ion detection in cells is a central topic in the bioanalytical and biomedical sciences with many potential impacts from human health and environmental protection (Xiong et al., 2020).

Given their weightiness, extensive efforts have been put to establish various methods for efficiently detecting ions, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Hwang and Jiang, 1996), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) (Wan et al., 2006), hyper-Rayleigh scattering (Kim et al., 2001), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Liu et al., 1999). In particularly, great efforts have been put to realize multiple ion detection using different strategies such as the fluorometric paper-based sensors (Sharifi et al., 2022; Feng et al., 2013), the electrochemical sensor based on a graphene aerogel and metal−organic framework composites (Lu et al., 2018), the ZnO nanorods field-effect transistors (FETs) array sensor (Bhat et al., 2021), single plasmonic nano sensors (Ye et al., 2022), and a multifunctional gel (Lai et al., 2022). These approaches possess great accuracies and sensitivity, but require sophisticated sample processing and expensive instruments, limiting their application in laboratory and industry (Li and Li, 2021). What is worse, these assays can not detect ions in living cells. Therefore, it is urgently required to develop novel types of effective and convenient strategies for ion detection, particularly in living cells.

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, presenting outstanding properties including fascinating optical traits, easy preparation and functionalization, low toxicity, great biocompatibility, and superior water solubility as compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (Yan et al., 2019). Thus, CDs have shown vast applications in bioimaging and biosensing (Ding et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2018a). In particular, tremendous efforts have been devoted to the development of CD-based fluorescent probes for the selective detection of metal ions such as Cd2+ (Gu et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2019), Cu2+ (Murugan et al., 2019), Hg2+ (Han et al., 2019), Zn2+ (da Silva J ú nior et al., 2021), Fe2+ (Wei et al., 2020), Fe3+ (Cheng et al., 2019), and anions like S2− (Sharma et al., 2018), and I− (Tang et al., 2021). Despite the great achievement on using CDs for ion detection, several challenges exist. First, the production of CDs is difficult to scale up due to the low product yield and difficult purification. Second, the mechanism behind the structure and function of CDs remains unclear, making it hard to realize the rational design and controllable preparation of CD-based ion probes. Third, Synthesis of CDs with fluorescence in the NIR-I (650–1000 nm) and NIR-II (1000–1700 nm) ranges is urgently needed yet challenging, which would improve tissue penetration and reduce autofluorescence. Generally, one type of CDs was fabricated to assay one single ion. Nevertheless, the achievement of multiple detection of two or more ions only with one sensor is highly desirable, due to the operation convenience, low cost, and saving of time. To date, CD-based fluorescence sensors designed for the detection of two or more kinds of ions have seldomly been reported (Li and Li, 2021). For example, Gao et al. developed the red emissive fluorescent C-dots for the multiple detections of noble metal ions (Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+) (Gao et al., 2018a). Yu, L et al. have fabricated dual-emissive fluorescent glutathione carbon dots (GSH-CDs) by a green hydrothermal method as a colorimetric fluorescent probe for the multi-detection of Zn2+, Mn2+, and Cu2+ simultaneously (Yu et al., 2018). Hu, G et al. have applied CDs derived from flax straw and EDA for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt (Co2+), chromium (Cr6+), and ascorbic acid (AA) (Hu et al., 2020). It is desirable to achieve the detection of multiple ions by one sensing platform that is highly sensitive with the capability to detect intracellular ions.

Herein, we propose an eco-friendly synthesis of carbon dots (mPD-CDs) for multicolor cell imaging and multiple ion sensing. mPD-CDs were simply prepared via one-step hydrothermal carbonization using m-phenylenediamine (mPD) as the precursor (Scheme 1 ). mPD-CDs can stain a broad range of cells like animal, bacterial, and fungal cells with no sample washing, excellent stability, and ultra-low toxicity. In addition, mPD-CDs was employed for ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, or I− due to the fluorescence enhancement, and Cd2+ and Zn2+ on the basis of fluorescence quenching effect. More interestingly, we achieved the multiple ion detection both in living cells and in zebra fish using mPD-CDs. Obviously, mPD-CDs present a potent platform for both universal cell imaging and multiple ion detection in vitro and in vivo, realizing one fluorescence dye for multiple purposes.

CDs (mPD-CDs) were fabricated from mPD using the facile, bottom-up solvothermal approach. Briefly, 100 mg mPD in 30 mL water was added into a 50-mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. The solution was incubated at 180 °C for 12 h before cooling down at room temperature. The supernatant was acquired by centrifuging the solution at 15,000 rpm for 15 min, which further went through a 0.22-μm filter membrane to discard large particles. The obtained CD sample was dialyzed for 1.5 h with milliQ water using a dialysis bag of 1000 Da cutoff, freeze-dried, and preserved at 4 °C for use in future.

The characterization of mPD-CDs were performed as described in our previous research, including the transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging, the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, the zeta potential value, the fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum, the fluorescence spectra, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) spectra, and the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) calculation (Lin et al., 2017, 2018a, 2018b; Durrani et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of varied ions on the fluorescence of mPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of 30 μg/mL mPD-CD was recorded before and after the addition of ion Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Na2+, or K+, at 340 nm under the excitation wavelength of 280 nm using a spectrofluorophotometer. The final ion concentration was 200 μM.

Aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Cd2+, or Zn2+) were added separately into 30 μg/mL mPD-CDs in water to reach different final ion concentrations: 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 130, 180, 300, 350, 450 and 500 μM for Cd2+; 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, and 50 μM for Cu2+; 0, 10, 20, 30, 40,50, 60, and 70 μM for Hg2+; 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 μM for Gd3+; 0, 5, 10, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, and 45 μM for Fe2+; 0, 20, 40, 60, 130, 180, 250, 350, 400, and 500 μM for Zn2+; 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 μM for Fe3+; 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM for I−. The fluorescence intensity of these mixtures were recorded at 340 nm with the excitation wavelength of 280 nm using a spectrofluorophotometer.

Raw 264.7, Hep G2, and 4T1 cells were cultivated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 utilizing complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (cDMEM) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum. The cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. After overnight growth, the culture medium in each well was replaced with 50 μg/mL mPD-CDs and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C.

Candida albicans (C. albicans) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae) were grown at 37 °C with 100 rpm in Yeast Malt (YM) medium (5 g/L peptone, 10 g/L glucose, 3 g/L yeast extract, and 3 g/L malt extract) and Yeast Extract Peptone Dextrose (YPD) medium (20 mg/mL peptone, 10 mg/mL yeast extract, and 20 mg/mL glucose), respectively. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), the Gram-negative and Gram-positive representative bacterium, respectively, were cultivated in LB medium (5 mg/mL yeast extract, 10 mg/mL tryptone, and 10 mg/mL NaCl) at 37 °C with 200 rpm overnight. The microorganism cells were collected by centrifugation and treated with 100 μg/mL mPD-CDs in the corresponding culture medium. After incubation for 1 h, the microorganism cells were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 3 min for confocal microscope imaging. Then, the cellular imaging was examined using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 63 × oil immersion objective. The confocal images were taken using blue, green, and red channel in the fluorescence emission range of 410–480 nm, 500–580 nm, and 581–700 nm upon the excitation at 405, 488, and 552 nm, respectively.

The PL intensity of mPD-CDs in water at various temperatures (37, 55, 70, and 90 °C) were recorded via spectrofluorophotometer at 340 nm with λex = 280 nm. Likewise, the PL intensityty of the CD samples at different KCl concentrations (0, 25, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μM) at room temperature were measured.

4T1 or L929 cells were cultivated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 utilizing complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (cDMEM) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum. With a density of 5 × 103 cells per well, cells were added in a 96-well plate, followed by overnight incubation. Then, the culture media was discarded and mPD-CDs were added at different final concentrations of 0, 40, 80, 120, 160, or 200 μg/mL in fresh culture media. After another 24-h incubation, the cell viability was assessed using standard MTT method.

The cytotoxicity of mPD-CDs on C. albicans and S. cerevisiae were assessed by recording the optical density value at 600 nm (OD600) via the microplate reader. Briefly, the overnight culture of C. albicans and S. cerevisiae was diluted 30 times using the corresponding culture medium, and then were grown until OD600 reached 0.5. Afterward, the log phase microorganisms were inoculated 1:10 into 150 μL fresh culture medium containing varied concentrations of mPD-CDs in the 96-well plate and cultivated for 12 h at 37 °C. Then, OD600 of each well was measured using a Multiskan FC microplate photometer (Thermo).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ion detection in animal cells and microorganisms using mPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hep G2 cells were cultivated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 utilizing complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (cDMEM) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum. With a density of 5 × 103 cells per well, cells were added in a 96-well plate, followed by overnight incubation. Then the overnight cultivated cells were incubated with different ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, or I−) for 2 h at 100 μM except Zn2+ which is 200 μM. Then the culture medium was replaced by 50 μg/mL mPD-CDs in cDMEM. After incubation for 30 min, the cells were imaged by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 63 × oil immersion objective. The fluorescence emission was 410 nm–480 nm upon the excitation at 405 nm. The detection of ions in microorganisms including S. cerevisiae were also performed in the same way. Besides, the flow cytometry was employed to record the cellular fluorescence intensity in different samples using FITC channel.

Zebrafish at 5 dpf (days post fertilization) were cultured at room temperature using standard zebrafish E3 culture medium (0.33 mM MgSO4.7H2O, 0.33 mM CaCl2, 0.17 mM KCl, and 5 mM NaCl) in 2 mL tubes. The zebrafish larvae were incubated with 200 μM Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+ or I− for 2 h before treating with 100 μg/mL mPD-CDs for 1.5 h. Then the confocal images of the zebra fishes were obtained on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 10 × objective. The fluorescence emission was 410 nm–500 nm upon the excitation at 488 nm.

mPD-CDs were fabricated from m-phenylenediamine (mPD) using a facile, bottom-up solvothermal method. TEM image (Fig. 1 A) and the corresponding histogram of size distribution (Fig. 1B) showed that mPD-CDs were in spherical form, uniform in size, and well monodispersed, featuring an average diameter of 8.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1C, the UV–vis spectrum of mPD-CDs exhibited an absorption band at 288.5 nm, which might be attributed to the
π −
π ∗
transition of the aromatic
sp 2
domains (Lin et al., 2019a). mPD-CDs was negatively-charged, displaying a charge of −5.71 mV as analyzed via zeta potential measurement (Fig. 1D). The fluorescence emission spectrum of mPD-CDs display an excitation-dependent emission in the excitation range of 260–420 nm (Fig. 1E), which has been commonly reported in the previous literature of CDs (Jiang et al., 2020a).

To investigate their composition, mPD-CDs were analyzed by FTIR and XPS. As displayed in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1F), the broad absorption bands around 3178 cm−1 and 3448 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of N–H (ν(N–H)) and O–H (ν(O–H)), respectively. The peak located at 2831 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H (ν(C–H)). The peak at 1601 cm−1 can be due to the CO stretching vibration (ν(CO)), probably resulting from the –COOH group or the amide bond (–CONH–). The two peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 1366 cm−1 may stem from the C–N stretching vibration (ν(C–N)), while the peak at 1122 cm−1 from the C–O–C stretching vibration (ν(C–O)). Moreover, the elemental components and surface groups of mPD-CDs were studied by XPS. mPD-CDs consisted of 68.5% carbon, 9% nitrogen, 22.5% oxygen elements. The full XPS spectra of mPD-CDs showed three peaks at 284.5 eV, 398.9 eV, and 532.8 eV for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1G). The C1s band contained three distinctive peaks that were related to carbon sp2 (CC, 283.9 eV), carbon sp3 (C–N, 284.8 eV), and carbon sp3 (C–O, 287.5 eV) (Fig. 1H). The N1s band was differentiated into three peaks at 398.5 eV for the pyridine N (C–N–C), 399.2 eV for the amino N (N–C), and 399.9 eV for the pyrrole N (C–NH2) (Fig. 1I). The two fitted peaks at 531.2 and 532.2 eV in the O1s spectrum can be owning to the oxygen element in CO and C–OH, and C–O–C forms, respectively (Fig. 1J). mPD-CDs exhibits high PL quantum yield of was 43% recorded under the excitation of 280 nm in water against quinine sulfate as a reference (Gao et al., 2018b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: mPD-CDs for multicolor imaging of animal, fungal and bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we checked whether mPD-CDs can serve as a multicolor imaging agent for cells. First, different types of animal cells such as the immune cells (Raw 264.7), and the cancer cells (Hep G2 and 4T1) were incubated with mPD-CDs and observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) using three different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 A). All the three tested animal cells displayed bright blue, green, and red fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 552 nm respectively, demonstrating that mPD-CDs can enter and stain animal cells. More interestingly, mPD-CDs were majorly accumulated in nucleoli in all three tested animal cells, resembling some CDs reported for staining nucleoli (Unnikrishnan et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020b). Furthermore, we investigated the bioimaging ability of mPD-CDs on fungal cells, utilizing the model fungi including C. albicans and S. cerevisiae (Fig. 2B). Both two types of fungi featured strong fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths after the treatment of mPD-CDs, showing that mPD-CDs can also applied to image fungal cells. Given that animal cells and fungal cells are both eukaryotic cells, we'd like to see if mPD-CDs can label prokaryotic cells. Thus, E. coli and S. aureus, the well-known Gram-negative and Gram-positive model bacterium respectively, were treated with mPD-CDs (Fig. 2C). Both tow bacteria showed great fluorescence in the presence of mPD-CDs (Fig. 2C), indicating that mPD-CDs can also stain prokaryotic cells. Although they have been well demonstrated to be a type of powerful nanomaterial for cell imaging, the currently available fluorescent CDs generally stain specific types of cells (Wang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2017; Fang et al., 2018a; Geng et al., 2019). One CD for all cells is rarely reported. Therefore, it is of great importance to find that mPD-CDs can stain a wide range of cells including both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. In addition, it is worth mentioning that all the confocal images of the tested cells were taken without sample washing. Altogether, these results demonstrate that mPD-CDs possess the excellent capability in wash-free multi-color cellular imaging for animal cells, fungi, and bacteria.

The photostability of mPD-CDs key for practical applications were evaluated under different experimental conditions including temperature (Fig. 6A), and ionic concentration (Fig. 3 B). The PL of mPD-CDs stayed constant with the elevated temperature, even up to 90 °C (Fig. 6A). Furthermore, the PL of mPD-CDs stayed unchanged with the treatment of different KCl concentrations (Fig. 3B). These results demonstrate that mPD-CDs possess an excellent PL stability, which would benefit their practical applications in ion detection and cell imaging.

Furthermore, to evaluate the safety of mPD-CDs, the relative viabilities of cancerous cells (4T1) and normal cells (L929), in the presence of mPD-CDs were evaluated by MTT assay (Fig. 3C). More than 90% of both 4T1 and L929 cell were alive with the treatment of mPD-CDs from 0 to 200 μg/mL, demonstrating that mPD-CDs are nontoxic within the concentrations (50 μg/mL) that are utilized for ion detection and cell imaging. Additionally, the cell viability of S. cerevisiae and C. albicans treated with up to 100 μg/mL mPD-CDs was not changed obviously in comparison to the untreated cells (Fig. 3D). Taken together, mPD-CDs display high PL stability and excellent biosafety, making them suitable for multiple ions detection in industry, in vitro, and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: mPD-CDs as the fluorescence probe for the detection of multiple ions
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different ions on the fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs was explored, including Cd2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Fe3+, I−, Na2+, K+, and Ag+. The ions fell into three groups according to their influences on the mPD-CDs fluorescence (Fig. 4 ). The first type of ions significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs, such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+ or I−. The second kind of ions notably reduced the fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs, like Cd2+, and Zn2+. In the presence of the third class of ions such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Na2+, and K+, the fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs stayed constant. This result showed the potential of mPD-CDs as fluorescence probes for the detection of multiple ions.

Therefore, mPD-CDs were explored to detect Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and I− based on their fluorescence enhancement of mPD-CDs, and Cd2+ and Zn2+ according to their quenching effect on the fluorescence of mPD-CDs (Fig. 5
). The linear detection range and the limit of detection (LOD) on the basis of 3σ/slope were calculated to be 5–40 μM and 3.7 nM for Cu2+, 10–70 μM and 3 nM for Hg2+, 10–70 μM and 5.4 nM for Gd3+, 5–45 μM and 3 nM for Fe2+, 1.5–8 μM and 3 nM for Fe3+, 5–100 μM and 9 nM for I−, 20–180 μM and 15 nM for Cd2+, and 20–500 μM and 61 nM for Zn2+. The linear ranges and LODs of mPD-CDs for these metal ions were comparable to that in the previously reported metal ion detection studies (Table S1) (Tang et al., 2019, 2021; Gu et al., 2018; Murugan et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2018b; Rong et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2017; Venkateswarlu et al., 2018; Zhang and Chen, 2014; Zhang et al., 2020; Iqbal et al., 2016; Lu and Zhou, 2019; Fang et al., 2018b; Yang et al., 2015; Du et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2019b; Guo et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of mPD-CDs for intracellular ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent cell imaging ability, high photostability, appealing biocompability of mPD-CDs, and the linear response of fluorescence intensity to ions, we applied mPD-CDs to detect intracellular ions in living cells. Hep G2 cells and S. cerevisiae were incubated with/without one of the eight kinds of ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Cd2+ or Zn2+) and then with mPD-CDs for confocal imaging (Fig. 6). It can be seen that the cells treated with Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, or I− exhibited markedly increased fluorescence as compared to those without the treatment with ions. In contrast, the animal cells or S. cerevisiae with the treatment of Cd2+ or Zn2+ ion exhibited significantly decreased fluorescence in comparison with those without the treatment of ions (Fig. 6A). These results demonstrated the capability of mPD-CDs in the intracellular detection of Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Cd2+, or Zn2+. In addition to CLSM, mPD-CDs can also detect multiple ions quantitatively in living cells through flow cytometry (Fig. 6B and C). The fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs in Hep G2 or S. cerevisiae treated with Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and I− individually were enhanced, while that in cells treated with Cd2+ or Zn2+ were reduced, which marched well with the confocal imaging findings (Fig. 6A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: mPD-CDs for sensing multiple ions in zebra fish
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we would like to examine whether the fluorescent mPD-CDs probe could be harnessed for ion detection in vivo. With this in mind, zebra fish were pretreated without/with each ion and then incubated with mPD-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of mPD-CDs in zebra fish was increased in the presence of Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, or I−, while decreased significantly in the presence of Cd2+ or Zn2+, as compared to that in zebra fish in the absence of any ions. The above result demonstrates that mPD-CDs can be deployed for probing multiple ions not only in living cells but also in living animals.

Our work focuses on the development of a CD-derived optical nanoprobe for multiple ions sensing in water and living cells, with high sensitivity and satisfactory biosafety. Using a single sensor for multiple ion detection offers advantages such as easy operation, time-saving, and low cost. This nanoprobe based on mPD-CDs has the potential application in situ environmental analyses, posing tremendously positive impact on human health and environmental monitoring and treatment. Research progress on CDs for sensing multiple ions are still only in their infancy, which leaves a huge space for future advancement.

To conclude, mPD-CDs were prepared by simple one step bottom-up hydrothermal method using a single precursor m-phenylenediamine. mPD-CDs can serve as a good fluorescent probe for imaging animal cells and microorganisms without sample washing, exhibiting great photostability and appealing biocompability. Furthermore, mPD-CDs was implemented for multiple ion assay with great sensitivity, including Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Gd3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, and I−. More importantly, mPD-CDs were successfully harnessed to monitor intracellular ions in living cells such as Hep G2 and S. cerevisiae, and in zebra fish. Based on our knowledge, it is the first time to apply CDs for fluorescence detection of Gd3+, atoning the lack of in-situ intracellular and in vivo Gd3+ assay. Additionally, the mPD-CDs is the first reporting probe which can detect Fe3+ as a “Turn-On” sensor, as far as we know. Generally, the CD-based sensors for Fe3+ detection are on the basis of quenching effect of Fe3+ on the CD fluorescence (Cheng et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2018b; Zhou et al., 2019). We envision that mPD-CDs will become an indispensable and powerful tool for sensitive measurement of ions in the environment and the biological systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32170072), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and Research Innovation Program of International Graduate Students of Southeast University (SEU).

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red emissive two‐photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular pH detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ye, Y. Xiang, Q. Wang, Z. Li, Z. Liu, A Red Emissive Two-Photon Fluorescence Probe Based on Carbon Dots for Intracellular pH Detection, Small 15(48) (2019) 1901673.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ye et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiplexed detection of heavy metal ions by single plasmonic nanosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ye, M. Yu, F. Wang, Y. Li, C. Wang, Multiplexed detection of heavy metal ions by single plasmonic nanosensors, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 196 (2022) 113688.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yu et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective determination of Zn2+, Mn2+, and Cu2+ simultaneously by using dual‐emissive carbon dots as colorimetric fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yu, G. Ren, M. Tang, B. Zhu, F. Chai, G. Li, D. Xu, Effective Determination of Zn2+, Mn2+, and Cu2+ Simultaneously By Using Dual-Emissive Carbon Dots as Colorimetric Fluorescent Probe, European Journal of Inorganic Chemistry 2018(29) (2018) 3418-3426.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zeng et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Orange-emissive sulfur-doped organosilica nanodots for metal ion/glutathione detection and normal/cancer cell identification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zeng, X.-W. Hua, Y.-W. Bao, F.-G. Wu, Orange-Emissive Sulfur-Doped Organosilica Nanodots for Metal Ion/Glutathione Detection and Normal/Cancer Cell Identification, ACS Applied Nano Materials (2021).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang and Chen, 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zhang, W. Chen, Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 55 (2014) 83-90.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, J. You, J. Wang, X. Dong, R. Guan, D. Cao, Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols, Dyes and Pigments 173 (2020) 107950.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of ferric ions and in a fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhou, G. Zhao, X. Tan, X. Qian, T. Zhang, J. Gui, L. Yang, X. Xie, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of ferric ions and in a fluorescent ink, Microchimica Acta 186(2) (2019) 67.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhu, H. Jin, C. Gao, R. Gui, Z. Wang, Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites, Talanta 162 (2017) 65-71.
